data.title: Navigating The Fog: 2026 And Timing The Market
data.description:
Historically, one of the challenges facing investors was gaining access to financial and market data to help them in their decision-making process. This has come full circle today, and the challenge today is sorting through an environment where there is simply too much information (TMI) available to investors with the advent of the internet.

Going forward, once or twice a month, this blog will highlight a research writing or two that I have recently encountered that readers might find of interest as well as timely.

With 2026 just getting underway, there isn't a shortage of forecast letters and commentary on expectations for the coming year. Some are more positive than others, and it is important to understand the pros and cons of a particular investment, but a recent strategy paper titled, The Baton is being Passed (PDF), is a worthwhile read as it highlights underlying issues impacting some market forces this year. The strategy paper (web link) is written by Ph.D. economist, James Thorne, Chief Strategist for Wellington-Altus Financial Inc. Some highlights are below:

  • In the short term, investors should ignore the fear of a massive U.S. midterm elections correction that dominates Wall Street narratives. Over the longer term, the world is entering a great rebalance in which the baton passes from a hollowed-out, financialized, post-Cold War order to a revived American system rooted in productive capital, manufacturing, and critical supply chains. With interest payments on debt exceeding military expenditures, there is no alternative to change; the current path is unsustainable.
  • In 2026, that baton pass becomes more visible as once controversial ideas - strategic tariffs, supply-side reform, running the economy hot - move from the fringe toward consensus, and the S&P 500’s intermediate-term path opens toward 14,000 by decade’s end.
  • None of this is risk-free: geopolitical shocks, energy price spikes, conflict, cyberattacks, premature policy clampdowns, uneven AI adoption, or a surging dollar destabilizing emerging markets could all trigger brutal repricing. These are risks to manage - not excuses to sit out.
  • In 2026, the familiar four-year market cycle begins to break down, returning control to the private sector and the underlying business cycle. Investors should expect risk-asset movements to be increasingly sentiment-driven, with prices often diverging from reality as fundamentals strengthen or weaken in ways the tape fails to reflect. Such disconnects should be expected during a generational innovation wave.

An underlying theme of the report is the economy is in a growth period and will be supported by a dovish Federal Reserve with its interest rate policy. A lower rate policy typically occurs in a slower growth economy, with the rate reductions creating stimulus that serves as a tailwind to economic growth. Markets do not move in a straight line, but this paper highlights reasons for a positive 2026 and the rest of the decade.

This next article by Oguz Erkan was only published on X (formerly Twitter) and highlights the difficulty in timing the market. Many famous investors, Warren Buffett, Peter Lynch and Bill Ackman say, "You can't time the market". One of Peter Lynch's famous quotes is "time in the market is more important than timing the market". The X post is titled 'Howard Marks: This is How You Can Time the Market'.

The theme of the article is one can't time the market but can take steps to position one's portfolio to have some cash available to put to work in a market pullback.

The overriding thought is the fact that the market does tend to move in cycles from undervalued to overvalued. We know this occurs with the economy as it cycles through slowdowns, recessions and then expansions. Similar cycles play out with the stock and bond market. As stated in the article:

  • There is nobody who can manage to become more successful with every step they take in their life. When you become very successful, the bar for success is raised so much that it becomes exponentially harder for you to pass it. This is the seed of failure in success. When the economy grows so much, it sets a high bar for itself to pass, and it inevitably fails to reach that bar. Once that happens, everything is ripe for a recession. Economic growth includes seeds of recession.
  • In the short-term debt cycle, the stock market follows the route from optimism & excitement to fear & panic, and then it repeats itself. This is the theoretical background of buying in fear and selling in euphoria.

As one reads the article, a conclusion is, "Timing when the cycles reach a turning point is impossible, but positioning yourself according to where you think we are in the cycles is possible and recommended."

In conclusion, the above paper and article seem timely. With 2026 and the rest of the decade appearing to be a favorable time period for investors, this comes on the back of three favorable returning years in the equity market.

Portfolio positioning can be important as recent returns in the equity market are indicative of one that is broadening out, and investors should be aware of this in the event one's portfolio is over or underweight certain asset classes or market segments during this transition.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Meta Is Investing Aggressively, And The Payoff Is Still Unclear
data.description:
Back in September, I rated Meta Platforms (META) a Hold when the stock was at $781.85. That call turned out to be cautious for good reason: the stock's dropped more than 20% since then and now sits near $620. Investors went from wild optimism about AI to picking stocks based more on fundamentals. Since then, the big changes have been another surge in infrastructure spending, Reality Labs still burning cash, and ad growth that's strong but not accelerating. The cautious take on valuation proved right. Now, with the stock trading at a more typical multiple, the risk and reward look pretty even.

Meta's still got a strong spot in AI and digital ads and can keep growing earnings and revenue by double digits, but the market's mistake is less obvious now. I think Wall Street is downplaying how much Meta's big spending will weigh on earnings the next couple years and probably not worrying enough about regulation or hardware risk in the estimates. At the same time, folks might be missing how much Meta's core ad business and rock-solid balance sheet can help if the AI bets pay off. So, I'm still neutral. The stock isn't cheap enough to buy, but the downside's limited since margins are strong and growth drivers are still there.

Meta still leads the pack in social media and online ads. Instagram now has 3 billion monthly users, and Threads has reached 150 million daily users. What really sets Meta apart is just how many people use its apps and how well its AI can target ads. Together, this supports a $60 billion annual run rate from AI-powered ad tools and a 26% jump in Family of Apps revenue, hitting $50.8 billion last quarter. Meta's going all in on building up its computing muscle, planning to spend tens of billions a year on AI infrastructure to lead in AI. All that spending helps support the rollout of new stuff like Meta AI, Vibes, and the latest smart glasses, bringing AI features to both regular users and businesses. But Reality Labs keeps bleeding money. $4.4 billion in Q3 alone, and it's nowhere close to breaking even. Moving staff from VR to AI shows management sees hardware isn't paying off yet and wants to focus on business areas that actually make money.

Digital ads still pay the bills at Meta, and global growth helps, but competition and new rules are making life tougher. Google, Amazon, and TikTok are all chipping away at market share. Margins are still top tier, but Meta makes less per user outside the US. Revenue could hit $235 billion in 2026 and nearly $273 billion in 2027, but every dollar is harder to win. Meta's going after huge new markets with AI for business messaging and open-source models, but it's got to actually make these bets work. Enterprise messaging is just getting started, and unlike Nvidia or Microsoft, Meta has to monetize its AI investments through its own products rather than by selling infrastructure directly. Meanwhile, new regulations, especially in Europe and the US/China standoff, are real risks for Meta's data use and international plans. China is now reviewing Meta's buyout of Manus, which adds another wildcard.

Meta's finances are still impressive. Family of Apps brought in $50.8 billion in sales and $25 billion in operating profit. Reality Labs? Another $4.4 billion loss. Net income hit $2.7 billion, pulled down by a one-time non-cash deferred tax adjustment of about $19 billion. But operating cash flow in the first nine months of 2025 was a huge $79.6 billion. That easily covers the heavy $50.1 billion in capex. Free cash flow is getting squeezed by all the spending and buybacks ($26.3 billion this year). The balance sheet is still rock-solid: $10.2 billion in cash, $34.3 billion in marketable securities, and long-term debt of about $29 billion. The ad business keeps churning out strong profits. Gross profit at 82% and net margins remaining strong once you strip out the one-time tax hit. Analysts see EPS at $30.38 in 2026 and $33.19 in 2027. Ads still bring in the most cash, with Asia-Pacific and Europe leading the way. Reality Labs keeps dragging things down, both for the numbers and investor mood. As Mark Zuckerberg put it on the Q3 call, Meta's betting big on compute now, aiming to be ready for a “generational paradigm shift” in AI.

At $620 a share, Meta's trading at about 20 times what analysts expect for 2026 earnings. That's not a bad price for a business still growing sales by double digits, with top margins and a real (if unproven) shot at more upside from AI. But this price also bakes in a lot of hope for AI and doubts about all the spending and Reality Labs' losses. If I put a 21x multiple on 2026 earnings, I get a fair value of $638, just 3% above today's price. A discounted cash flow model points to the same numbers, $641 a share. If you're more optimistic and give Meta a higher multiple, say 23x to 24x, because of its stronger margins and growth, that gets you to around $700 to $730, especially if its AI bets or hardware really start to take off. But if ads slow down or new stuff they're pushing doesn't land, the stock could easily fall to $550 to $570.

Investor mood has cooled off since the AI hype peak. Now, it's more wait-and-see. Most of the news on Meta is about big headlines, like the nuclear energy deal or new exec hires, but investors just want to see when all these AI investments turn into real revenue. Wall Street didn't get too excited about the last earnings. Shares dipped a bit as people focused on Reality Labs' ongoing losses and big spending, even though the core business did well. I read that as the market realizing Meta's business is solid, but not bulletproof. The era of just trusting the big tech names is over. Now Meta has to prove it deserves the price.

Meta's biggest headaches: regulators (especially in Europe), actually pulling off all this AI investment, and tough competition from Google, Amazon, TikTok, and Apple. Reality Labs keeps burning cash, and if Meta's shift away from it doesn't work out, annual cash flow could drop by a few billion, and the stock could take a hit. If Europe cracks down and cuts Meta's sales there by 10% and if capex jumps another $10 billion without more revenue, EPS could drop by $3 to $4 a year. That could drop the stock to about $550. Meta is spending more on AI, buying energy, and buying back its own shares to handle this, which makes sense, but there's no promise it'll work.

What matters next is pretty simple. Meta needs to keep ad revenue climbing, show that AI business messaging really delivers, and prove that all this AI spending actually boosts profits by 2027. Meta says more people will keep using its platforms and clicking on ads, but analysts aren't so sure sales or profits will keep growing as fast. The big things to watch are whether Reality Labs starts losing less money, whether business messaging actually turns into a real money-maker, and if Meta eventually shifts from heavy building mode to focusing more on profits. If hardware ends up being a hit or regulations loosen up, the stock could move higher. But if margins get squeezed or new rules make life harder, the shares could head the other way.

Bottom line: Meta is still a Hold at this price. The ad business is solid, profits are strong, and if the AI bets work, there's room to grow. But with all this spending, Reality Labs losing money, and more regulation, the stock looks priced about right. I'd only call it a Buy if Meta proves the AI projects are really working or if the price drops low enough to be a good deal. For now, it makes sense to wait.

data.title: Lucky Strike Entertainment: Too Uncertain For 2026 (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
In this article, I'll provide an update on Lucky Strike Entertainment Corporation (LUCK), which was previously called Bowlero, an entertainment company in the bowling sector. The company has, since my last article, seen a decline and underperformance relative to both the market and other investments. If we follow valuation principles, this should make the company more attractive with a better upside, but instability in the company, coupled with the instability in the market, as well as the lack of forecast accuracy, has created uncertainty in what the company is and what it offers. How, you may ask?

The current estimated upside forecasts give the company positive numbers, but these numbers come with some of the worst historical accuracy of any coverage I've previously done. The company also isn't that "old" on the market; it hasn't been around for more than a few years.

My previous article on the company can be found here, and it was back in 2024. The rebrand it has gone through since about a year back includes, aside from an entirely new name for the company, also new services and targets. This is reflected by the change in name - Bowlero is obviously the name for a bowling-oriented company, which this company no longer is.

Its brand portfolio has expanded, some with bowling brands, but the company has also expanded into water parks and family entertainment, with brands like Raging Waters, Wet 'n Wild, and Boomer Parks.

This is a different business model with different targets than the one that I covered, and I now say that it warrants different estimates and considerations.

The stock price decline, meanwhile, is (I believe) based on increased debt and use of cash due to, among other things, significant M&A investments. It has generated negative free cash flow margins, which I would say works to convey doubt about what the company is able to do here.

But there are more issues that we need to visit before giving the company an update on what I consider the most important metric, it's price target.

Visibility for the new strategy is low

We have 1Q26 results. These were released in November 2024.

As usual, I'll start with the positives. The company is the undisputed NA market leader in what I'll call "bowling consolidation" and "bowling entertainment". It has a wide selection of venues and assets, and provided that bowling remains attractive, it has solid growth potential. Company management argues for the advantages of continued M&A and the upsides that this strategy brings to the company, and because of its high debt, it focuses on its relevant track record of delevering the company over time, which it has done before in its history.

The management team is also experienced. Revenue growth over time has been impressive. LUCK has managed 10% or above in CAGR during the past few years.

Its M&A-oriented growth is proven. Proven how, you may ask? LUCK has done acquisitions from AMF and Brunswick, and it offers significant diversification.

On paper, the growth in revenues, even on a per-location basis on average, is impressive. It has managed over 5% CAGR over time. The company has, in FY25, recorded a significant increase in operational cash flow. Also, a meaningful decrease in Capital expenditures. Looking at this, the company's trends are positive. This is despite an extensive current "rebranding" initiative that involves the company creating a new look for many of its locations. This is set to be finished by the end of 2026.

Let's put it like this: In a scenario where you believe that the company's assets and business areas are likely to experience either growth or upside, it's understandable if you have a positive outlook on LUCK. The business model for the bowling assets includes more "upscale" sort of locations with higher-quality food than is typically found in such venues. Also, if the company was bowling only, then I'd probably be more positive about the prospects than I am.

So, we have some issues. Let me show you what they are.

First off, I want to make it clear that the combination of the two business models (water parks and bowling) isn't necessarily as good as some analysts (this includes me) might have thought initially. Water parks are seasonally focused. They have significant margins, a very varied demographic, and significant non-core incomes from merchandise. A bowling alley meanwhile, to clearly put the two side by side, is lower margin, depending on lane rentals, all around, and has a different scale and target audiences. I'd argue that Bowling is more "activity", where Water park is more of an "experience destination". Trying to combine these business models could lead to challenges that need to be taken into consideration.

In my last article, I gave the company a positive "BUY" rating. I also made it clear that I wasn't going to add more than a small position in the case of buying. I ended up not buying the company. I regret, at this point, the "BUY" rating I gave. It underestimated the challenges and risks for the company while also allowing too much leeway for what was essentially no more than a speculative business.

One of the advantages for the time being is that the company has a real estate portfolio. This acts as a bit of a financial backing to its plans. But does this take away its risks or problems on an operational level? I'd say that 'no' it doesn't.

In the past 3 years, I've become much less forgiving about companies with high debt. LUCK one unfortunately has a lot of debt. Its strategy is to "roll-up" existing businesses. This means purchasing small independent assets (or struggling ones), which in turn works from the assumption that these assets are attractive to begin with. It also assumes that LUCK is able to monetize and improve upon these assets.

If you think this sounds like a risky assumption, that's because it is. It's especially so when considering the characteristics of the real estate that the company gets involved with. What I mean by this is that the real estate is non-fungible. Calling it that means it can only be used for what it was originally used and built for.

Sure, if you remove lanes and machinery, you might be able to get a "box" that you use for other ventures. But a water park is a water park. You must also consider location. The economics of bowling tend to ensure that their placement is typically in less-than-attractive areas. This impacts the resale value of that particular piece of real estate.

What I am talking about here is the underlying safety for what LUCK does. I believe this to be low.

We can also get into issues like the company's pricing for services. Some LUCK locations are now charging upwards of $8-$10/game, and in today's society and economy, I have an easy time seeing a situation where players elect not to go for that cost. Consider for a moment the wide array of options we have available to us today.

I believe that only proven long-term positive results and trends would justify a high-conviction "BUY" rating for this company. I don't see this here. Current forecasts for LUCK include growth in 2026-2026E but followed by declines in 2028E. I've also told you about the poor forecast accuracy. This is another reason why I believe the company could underperform, at least from a P/E perspective.

Let's see the valuation.

Tricky, despite a decline since 2024

The double-digit share price decline in a company might typically imply a "BUY". I can also make the argument that due to high debt levels, significant expansion costs, and a clear reliance on non-cash expense levels (which lower net income), the use of P/E as a metric for the company might not be the best idea.

What I'll do instead is use P/FFO or an EV/EBITDA rating. The latter reintroduces interest, tax, and D&A, which focus on operational profitability, which could be more important.

On the basis of either EV/EBITDA or P/FFO/OCF, I can see a different picture in results but not in forecast accuracy. LUCK still misses most of the time, even with a 10-20% margin of error.

The company has a yield. This means you're getting paid while you wait, and there's a clear argument here to be made as to why the company could go up with time. Forecasts and company results show some potential for this.

The reason I argue against this is the decline and the risks mentioned below. The lower share price conveys a lack of confidence in the company's strategy as well as focus by the market.

LUCK has moved from above 12x P/OCF to less than 8x. It trades at 7x P/OCF. That's relatively cheap for the cash flows that the company is generating here, but the very low accuracy and potential issues would justify it. What also justifies it is the short company operating history. I used more P/E multiples in my previous article. For reasons stated above, I'm using OCF here.

My previous PT was $16/share based on a standard fair-value 15x P/E. I'm switching to a historically discounted (heavily due to its uncertainty and lack of forecastability) 6-8x OCF range. Even with the estimated growth, which implies a fair value of about $12-14/share, I'm lowering my PT to $8/share for the company. This PT is still speculative though.

That's an upside of about 7.5x OCF. This is the 25% discounted 4-year average, which I now believe to be the best metric that we have for the company. This target reflects debt risk, execution risk, and the risk to its varied business model. I don't believe the assets the company operates are as low risk as some argue. Given the consumer pricing trends I see it becoming more difficult to convince consumers to go to these venues, not less. This is confirmed in the numbers, which show a decline in customers, not an upswing, despite same-store revenue growth.

I believe my new PT to be a better representation of the upside and potential the company has for the reasons stated above.

Risks & Considerations

I find risks in the company's mixed business model, the value (both residual and current) of its real estate, and also the appeal of what it actually operates or buys. This means that I've changed my view from my last article and view this as a much-higher risk sort of business. My new PT is more risk-adjusted than before to these issues that I see.

Things like consumer pricing levels, sentiment, and potential company appeal are central to the company's potential here. The fact that revenue is going up, while traffic is going down shows me a worrying trend. It tells me that the company is extracting more money per customer, but from a smaller total number of customers.

While this may be a positive in some situations, this is not telecommunications. I believe the risk is that LUCK may be, for lack of a better wording, "pushing its luck", and we may see further drops that eventually result in significant revenue declines.

My updated thesis is as follows.

Thesis

Lucky Strike Entertainment is a potentially interesting company, even apart from owning the company's Landlord VICI properties. I do believe that LUCK could be fundamentally attractive in 2026. But I also believe this company comes with significantly more risk than I estimated over a year ago. Forecast accuracy and age remain the problem here. I say the problem is now worse than it was before.

LUCK continues to show a very low historical and forecast-based chance of successfully hitting targets and is currently an EPS-negative business. It should be said that OCF and EBITDA are better ways to value and look at the company. When basing forecasts on this, then the upside is better.

I'm changing my rating and my PT for this company and the way I value it. My new estimate is about half of what it was before. This also reflects the current overvalued state of the market. It now comes in at about $8/share in terms of price target.

For this updated PT, I'm allowing the company to trade at around 7-8x OCF, and I'm forecasting at 2026E-2027E. This would allow upside. Remember though, this company is a speculative play. In this market, I would be very careful with speculative plays (it's too volatile).

I may end up adding the company at a lower price because I like the business at my targeted price, and I like management (and still do here for 2026). However, this is not one for the faint-hearted, so be careful as to how much you buy and if you buy.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is overall qualitative.

  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.

  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.

  • This company is currently cheap.

  • This company has a realistic upside based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators, and 0 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 3/5 "stars" here, which typically isn't enough. Based on this, and a price target of $8/share where, I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me. I give the company a "Hold" rating as of January 2026.

data.title: Politics And The Markets 01/20/26
data.description:
This is the forum for daily political discussion on Seeking Alpha. A new version is published every market day.

Please don't leave political comments on other articles or posts on the site.

The comments below are not regulated with the same rigor as the rest of the site, and this is an 'enter at your own risk' area as discussion can get very heated. If you can't stand the heat... you know what they say...

More on Today's Markets:

Moderation Guidelines:

We remove comments under the following categories:

  • Personal attacks on another user account
  • Anti-Vaxxer or covid related misinformation
  • Stereotyping, prejudiced or racist language about individuals or the topic under discussion.
  • Inciting violence messages, encouraging hate groups and political violence.

Regardless of which side of the political divide you find yourself, please be courteous and don't direct abuse at other users.

For any issue with regards to comments please email us at : moderation@seekingalpha.com.

Seeking Alpha's Disclosure: Past performance is no guarantee of future results. No recommendation or advice is being given as to whether any investment is suitable for a particular investor. Any views or opinions expressed above may not reflect those of Seeking Alpha as a whole. Seeking Alpha is not a licensed securities dealer, broker or US investment adviser or investment bank. Our analysts are third party authors that include both professional investors and individual investors who may not be licensed or certified by any institute or regulatory body.

data.title: Wipro: Consider Above-Consensus Earnings And Muted Prospects
data.description:
I've kept Wipro Limited (WIT) as a Hold-rated name.

Its 3QFY2026 (YE March 31) earnings beat had a favorable read-across regarding its margins. But the enterprise's forward-looking revenue projections suggest that growth prospects are lackluster.

Its 2nd quarter showing and management's Q3 guide were evaluated in the prior October 17, 2025 write-up.

Normalized Bottom line Was A Positive Surprise

WIT's numbers for the latest three-month period were disclosed last Friday morning.

Its 3QFY26 core EPS was ₹3.4. I'm impressed with the +5% (source: S&P Capital IQ) outperformance versus consensus.

The firm's analyst call highlighted non-recurring items like "gratuity expenses due to implementation of the new labor code" and a "restructuring exercise that was completed."

WIT saw its sales go up +6% year-on-year to ₹0.24T in the recent quarter. That's consistent with the sell-side's prediction of INR 235B.

The group's better-than-anticipated profitability led to a Q3 beat.

According to S&P Capital IQ data, WIT's 3QFY2026 EBIT-to-sales of 17.6% exceeded the analysts' forecast by 30 bps (basis points). This was equivalent to YoY and sequential improvements of +0.1ppts (percentage points) and +0.4ppts, respectively.

My take is that WIT's margin-enhancement measures paid off.

The proportion of offshore revenues rose from 3QFY25's 60.8% to 3QFY26's 61.6%. This income stream is high-margin, leveraging lower-cost locations to execute on projects.

WIT has also pivoted away from low-return cost-plus contracts. At the media briefing, the company emphasized the importance of "fixed price programs" to boost "profitability."

Employee expenses-to-topline declined -210 bps QoQ and -140 bps YoY to 57.9% in 3QFY2026. The press conference commentary noted that "our recruitment from campuses was muted." Avoiding the urge to go on a hiring spree was rewarding.

Looking ahead, the market expects WIT's EBIT-to-revenues to be 16.8% in FY27 representing a +0.4ppts increase. I think that's realistic, considering the potential to cut costs post-acquisition.

Its staff utilization deteriorated from 2QFY26's 86.4% to 3QFY2026's 83.1%. This came after the takeover of the IT engineering business "Harman DTS/HDTS" in Dec last year.

I see the above-mentioned metric dropping in the future. You would assume that WIT will rationalize the workforce and make existing employees more efficient upon HDTS's successful integration.

The firm has the track record to back this up. It indicated at the Q3 presser that "there was a rebadging of employees" at the previous "Phoenix (M&A) deal."

WIT's stock price fell 7% on the results release day (Jan 16). Investors are clearly worried about its future, notwithstanding its above-consensus EPS.

But Near-Term Expectations Are Underwhelming

Management anticipates that its 4QFY26 turnover will widen by +1.0% (mid-point) quarter-on-quarter.

The sell side was projecting a superior +1.3% growth instead.

To make things worse, the organic guidance, excluding HDTS's contributions, is estimated to be -0.5%.

WIT attributed the uninspiring outlook to "certain delayed ramp-ups in some of the large deals that we won earlier" at the analyst briefing.

My mid-Oct article cautioned that "mega-projects are generally more complex", translating into a "longer timeline for initial kick-start and eventual completion."

The 3Q earnings call also stated that "our clients" are focused on adding "AI capabilities" and "advanced transformational projects." My worry is that WIT's assignments are deemed to be of lower-priority. This raises the probability of further timeline slippage.

There are also signs of negative growth momentum.

WIT's total bookings fell -5% YoY to $3.3B for 3QFY26.

The "large deal" sub-segment's "Total Contract Value/TCV" for the third quarter this fiscal year amounted to $871M. That translated into a -9% decrease.

Note the figures cited above are in US dollars, in contrast with its reporting currency in Indian rupees.

Separately, some of the economically sensitive verticals are still under pressure.

At the media conference, WIT acknowledged that "consumer" and "manufacturing" are "a little bit impacted with trade and tariffs." The management's expectations are that "uncertainties there continue."

My Conclusion

I still have a Neutral view of this stock.

There are both good and bad takeaways from WIT's financial disclosures. You'll be encouraged by its profitability uptrend. But I've misgivings about the company's growth profile

It currently trades at 20 times consensus next twelve months' P/E. That's on par with its Indian counterpart Tata's (TCS.NS) forward EPS multiple of 20.8x.

I've judged WIT's shares to be fairly valued.

A 'Hold' rating is also appropriate given its stockholder returns. Its FY27 dividend yield is 3.7% (source: S&P Capital IQ), is decent. Investors can enjoy cash distributions while waiting for deal-to-revenue conversion to materialize.

data.title: Nike: Too Early To Call A Comeback
data.description:
In terms of brand image, I have always believed that Nike (NKE) would dominate the sports shoe market and it would be among the most well known brands globally. I remember how it influenced me in my youth, the film Forrest Gump and his iconic Nike Cortez. However, something broke last time, and the marketing lost its strength. Over the last two years, Nike went from third place in the brand ranking elaborated by Best Global Brands, to place number 23rd in 2025.

Beyond good or bad business decisions, market value, or stock price, companies have their own marketing strategies. And I'm sure Nike is no longer the way it used to be seen. If you ask me for an example of good marketing, I think of American Eagle Outfitters (AEO) and its publicity with Sydney Sweeney. Anyway, I'm not a woman who works in marketing or advertising, so I'm completely ignorant on the subject and won't comment further.

What I like is to look at balance sheets, consumer trends, valuation metrics, and profitability. So, let's go with that.

Profitability problems

I briefly reviewed Nike's declining profitability over the past ten years. It's not that I want to delve deeper into my analysis of past performance. In fact, I want to look forward. But because the company's stock has been declining, I simply want to illustrate this trend with a chart.

In Q2 26, presented in December, showed an EBIT margin of 6.16% over the last 12 months, the lowest level in the series, even below the pandemic drop. There was a recovery attempt in 2024, which reached its historical average, but then it fell sharply again to its current low. This latest drop also occurred in an environment where the market had been pricing in the Fed's rate-cutting cycle throughout 2025. Even so, the positive expectations weren't enough. I haven't forgotten the subject of tariffs, which also impacted the company's margins, but I'll talk about that later, in the risks section, given that they are still an obstacle to the 2026 projections.

So, I still have one question to answer. What was the cause of Nike's decline? In cases like this, the causes are varied and lie in the chosen business model. The DTC strategy, the overly aggressive move away from physical stores, its break with retail chains, and serious inventory problems are among the main reasons for the low profitability.

Now, when I look at the future, I am tempted to see a floor in the profit margins. And there's nothing I like more than a floor on a chart to buy. Nevertheless, I think there's a lack of fundamentals here, and I feel it's too early to predict a rebound. I believe the company has already understood its mistakes, and the CEO himself, Elliott Hill, explains on every call that they're in a transition process. Righting the ship, in other words.

Fiscal year '26 continues to be a year of taking action to rightsize our Classics business, return NIKE Digital to a premium experience, diversify our product portfolio, deepen our consumer connections, strengthen our partner relationships and realign our teams and leadership. And I'd say we're in the middle innings of our comeback.

Source: Q2 26 Earnings Call Transcript

Good signals and improvements I'm expecting for 2026

What evidence am I waiting for to be more optimistic about Nike? Profitability, of course. And for that, I'll need to see continued growth in the wholesale segment, good inventory management, and a return to net profit growth, at the very least.

The good news is, precisely, that the wholesale segment grew 8% YoY, reaching $7.5 billion, and I'm sure this is a recovery signal, while it's expected that it consolidates in the following quarters. This growth coexists with the DTC segment, which dropped 8% YoY until reaching $4.6 billion (slide 9, 10-Q). But I'm not really worried about this segment, unless it drops too much. I do care about seeing a consolidation of the wholesale segment. Total revenue is also important, but it's not yet rebounding strongly: it grew only 0.6% YoY in the quarter.

Another metric I'm paying attention to is inventory management. In Q2 2026, inventory turnover was 0.96x, the best ratio since 2021, as you can see in the chart below. Regarding this metric, I can see the company's efforts to be more efficient, better controlling costs and preventing mismatches between stock and demand.

Inventory went from $8.1 billion in Q1 2026 to $7.7 billion in Q2 2026, and if it maintains this turnover rate, or even exceeds it in the coming quarters, I think I can be more confident in better management in this area.

What I would also like to see is that net income stops falling. This didn't happen only in the last quarter, but it is part of the general decline over the last few years. Net income in Q2 26 was $792 million, which represented a 32% YoY decrease. In the following chart, you can see the quarterly trend of the last 5 years of earnings. Returning to profits above $1 billion per quarter, I think, will be a primary objective.

The catalyst for the World Cup in a challenging environment

I believe I understand that the company is going through a transition process, and both profits and profit margins will take time to recover to their past levels. I think 2026 will be a decisive year for better understanding this transition because there will be a key catalyst: the FIFA World Cup, which will be played from June 11 to July 19.

In the massive world of sporting events, Nike has a strategic brand position, and in fact, it will have Cristiano Ronaldo of the Portuguese National Team as its brand ambassador. However, there are two factors that lead me to be more cautious. The first is that Nike has already lost the leadership in sponsoring national team jerseys to adidas (ADDYY) ahead of the World Cup. Nike was the leader in 2022, but now it is in second place.

The other factor that's calling my attention is changes in consumer behavior regarding sports footwear. Based on the analysis of Modaes, sportswear is no longer as prevalent in users' wardrobes as it once was, and although major events continue to be catalysts, these may only involve temporary leaps. In other words, consumption trends in the long term remain the same: more selective customers and harder to capture as fans. This second factor involves other traditional brands, such as adidas or Puma (PMMAF), as the main competitors in the World Cup market.

China's challenge

Although I've tried to explain that the DTC segment is not something I'm worried about because I'm focused on the wholesale segment, the mistakes in this segment are still causing some negative effects in the Chinese market. The lack of investment in Chinese retail outlets continues to affect Nike's revenue, with a 36% drop in its digital sales in the country this quarter. In the first two quarters of the fiscal year 2026, China's revenue fell 13%, and only in the last quarter did it fall 17% (slide 31, 10-Q). By the end of fiscal year 2025 (last July), China's revenues had also fallen 13% year-on-year and 12% in local currency (slide 41, 2025 report annual).

It's true that consumption in China is a little weaker than before, and that's why the Chinese government is boosting consumer subsidies for 2026. I must admit I'm not a fan of consumption subsidies, and even more so when this is not China's first subsidy plan (it's the fifteenth one!). Anyway, this could be favorable news in the short term for clothing brands.

Even so, when I reviewed adidas' balance sheets in China, I discovered that this is not a problem of weak consumption, but rather an obstacle specific to Nike. During Q3, Adidas reported revenues similar to those in China for the same period in 2024 and a 6% increase in local currency.

Adidas's resilience, just to mention one example, reveals that a foreign brand in China is able to succeed. There are some local brands, such as ANTA (ANPDY) and Li Ning (LNNGY), that increase competition, but if Adidas can overcome them, why can't Nike? Part of that transition process back to the big leagues will also be seen in the adaptation to the Chinese market.

Valuation

Despite all the difficulties, risks and challenges, Nike is still the company with the largest market share globally, at almost 20% of the sector. Moreover, Nike is the company with the largest market capitalization in the footwear segment, and the fourth largest if I include the clothing, accessories, and luxury items segment (see comparison).

So therefore, I consider that such a position gives the company high valuation multiples compared to the sector, such as the EV/EBITDA (25x), which more than doubles the sector median (12x). That's the reason why I paid more attention to the fact that the average multiples of the last 5 years were expected to be higher. But they weren't. The average EV/EBITDA of the last 5 years was approximately 25x.

Moreover: the stock is trading at 37 times its earnings, compared to an average of 34 times over the last 5 years. So I came to the conclusion that the market is still rewarding the leadership position of Nike, despite all the difficulties. This also implies, in my view, that the market is pricing in some of the positive signs from the last quarter, such as the growth of the wholesale segment and the improvement in inventory management. So, I believe the stock is totally overvalued.

If that's the case, and if my perspectives were optimistic, I could close with a buying qualification. However, as I've explained before, I still see some important challenges. And I was seeing this in the growth projections, both for revenue, EBITDA, EBIT, and earnings per share. While Nike is expected to experience a 1.8% drop in revenue, Adidas could grow by 9.6%, Deckers Outdoor Corporation (DECK) by 10.3%, and On Holdings (ONON) by 30.9% (see comparison).

Tariff risks

Fiscal year 2026 will be harder than expected regarding tariffs because the company estimates an annualized impact of $1.5 billion, with a notable impact on gross margin. In Q2 26, tariffs represented a loss of 500 bps in the gross margin, although the final decline was 330 bps. For Q3 26, based on what they said in the call, they are expecting a smaller but equally important impact: 315 bps, ending with a loss of 175 to 225 bps.

But let's go back for a moment to one of the questions I asked at the beginning of this article. Profitability is far to reach, even discounting the lower impacts of tariffs. Furthermore, the issue of tariffs, which I believe has already been factored into the price for months, is not only on Nike's imported products, but also on manufacturing in Vietnam, where the company makes 51% of its footwear (slide 6, 2025 report). Vietnam's tariffs were set at 20%, although initially they were 46%.

Based on these profitability problems in the future due to tariffs, I prefer a Hold rating rather than Buy. I could invest without a problem in companies with weak profit margins, but I need to see prospects for improvement. I don't feel very comfortable when the horizon is still a risk. Sadly, Nike still has future tariff risks. If I had a position in Nike, I would stay invested and wouldn't sell, at least for now. I believe the company is on the right path, and the valuation is fair.

Summary

Throughout the article, I tried to explain my view on Nike, a company I consider to be a sleeping giant that's trying to get back to its glory days. I can see some positive signals. Among them I highlighted the improvement in inventory management and the growth in the wholesale segment. But I also found some challenges that made a long term investment decision inadvisable. These included declining net income, declining profit margins, the challenges of the Chinese market, and tariff risks. That's why I'm rating the company as a Hold.

I also wanted to briefly explain my perspective on the World Cup, which I consider is a transient catalyst. But in an environment of some decline for the brand, Nike lost its lead to Adidas in shirt sponsorships, which for me is a sign of regression. Even so, I didn't rule out seeing revenue improvements thanks to such an event, but structural consumer trends are no longer rewarding large, traditional sports brands.

So, for a transition like the one Nike's going through, that diminishes the catalyst. If the challenges and risks analyzed aren't addressed, I think it's too early to predict a rebound in the share price. Besides, I believe the market shares my view on Nike; that's the reason why it's fairly valued at current prices.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: PLS Group Limited: The Easy Money Is Gone (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Introduction

Being a non-US listed stock and only available on the OTC exchange, it is no wonder that PLS Group Limited (Pilbara Minerals Ltd.) (PILBF) is a stock with very low coverage on Seeking Alpha. Therefore, my latest analysis on it is the most recent one, despite being over three months old.

In summary, my thesis was the following

  • Lithium prices have increased over the last few months again, prompting an improved investor sentiment and leading to new technical uptrends in producers like Pilbara Minerals.
  • The Australian exploration company boasts operational efficiency, with operating unit costs down, CAPEX down, and production up.
  • Macroeconomic, technical, and fundamental factors support longer-term lithium price appreciation, from which Pilbara is poised to benefit exponentially, though uncertainties about prices still remain and the firm is currently losing money.
  • They trade at a fair valuation, making me initiate the stock at a Buy.

This thesis has been playing out so far, as the stock has rallied 73% since my coverage, almost reaching 2023's all-time highs.

It is therefore fair to say that the sentiment around Pilbara has improved significantly, and investors now expect the company to soon report large amounts of profits again, similar to FY23 (July 2022 until June 2023). The market capitalization is higher than during said peak, likely due to dilution.

All of the recent gains show that the financial community (as Phil Fisher would describe it) is appraising the stock with higher multiples thanks to better future prospects. And in part, this is deserved, as lithium prices have actually risen, the company continues to improve operational efficiency, and the long-term lithium thesis remains intact.

News on Lithium price development from Trading Economics:

Lithium carbonate futures surged past CNY 150,000 per tonne in January, gaining nearly 30% since the start of the year to a two-year high, as signs of strong demand for power storage coincided with the outlook of capped supply. Chinese authorities lowered export rebates for battery producers from April, driving manufacturers to front-run lithium orders. In the meantime, ambitious bets in power and datacenter infrastructure by the Chinese government was combined with the announcement of higher power storage spending, supporting the outlook for lithium and other battery metals. This was combined with Beijing stating it would double EV charging capacity to 180 gigawatts by 2027, supporting lithium-rich energy storage systems. Meanwhile, authorities stated 27 mining permits were canceled in the lithium hub of Jiangxi, aligned with the earlier suspension of activity in CATL's Jianxiawo lithium mine amid Beijing's anti-involution campaign.

Today, I am arguing that Pilbara Minerals has gotten slightly ahead of itself and existing shareholders should take some profits, as I have done as well. Risks are now becoming more pronounced as the valuation and thereby future expectations for profits have risen drastically despite volatile lithium prices. My long-term thesis remains intact, which is why I recommend some profit-taking and holding on to the rest of the shares.

Financial Update

Since my last coverage, Pilbara has reported their September quarter figures.

  • Production at 224.8kt is up 2% YoY and QoQ, respectively
  • Volume sold is at 214kt, essentially flat YoY
  • Revenue grew 19.5% to A$251M
  • Unit operating costs are down 10% YoY and 11% QoQ

However, further drops in operating costs might come to an end from here:

While the September Quarter delivered strong cost performance, unit costs are expected to face upward pressure over the remainder of the financial year due to seasonal operational challenges typically associated with the wet season, and the continued implementation of end-to-end optimisation initiatives, including increased processing of contact ore.

Valuation

The expansion is clearly visible in my previously used P/B and P/S metrics, which are much better than TTM P/E ratios due to PILBF's unreliable profitability. Their P/B has risen to 4.3, which is not a level I would consider to be dangerous, but one that does start to warrant some caution.

Similarly, their P/S multiple is now almost at 19. Looking at these figures again, I believe we are fairly valued to maybe slightly overvalued.

Leveraging their highly scalable operations, Pilbara is able to achieve 50-60% net income margins during phases of very high lithium prices. Let's assume, thanks to their efficiency and cost improvements, they can have 65% net income margins. At revenues of $4B, that would translate to after-tax profits of $2.6B. This is slightly higher than what they were able to report in July 2022–June 2023, which is their fiscal year 2023. At that time, however, the market was only awarding Pilbara a P/E multiple of 6 given the incredibly rapid spike in fundamentals and its cyclicality. It was pricing in revenues and profits, having found a top. If we now take our $2.6B and multiply it by 6, we arrive at a potential maximum market cap of $15.6B. This tells us we have around 50% more potential upside ahead of us for this stock under these assumptions. Of course, in a more aggressive lithium cycle with inflation persisting, Pilbara could also earn way more than ever before. But we are not seeing that, at least for now.

Another important aspect of my initial analysis was based on the charts, complementing fundamental analysis.

Today, I believe the charts show a mixed bag. Overall, the uptrend remains intact, prompting higher future prices, as price remains above all major moving averages (10, 50, 200) on the weekly chart.

Reaching old highs could be a firm resistance. The RSI seems to struggle to create new highs, and a bearish cross on the weekly chart could indicate downside from here. The same formation marked the 2018 top, but in 2022, it only marked the beginning of a much longer rally. The same can be seen on the monthly RSI, where the stock is inching closer to overbought territory, similar to the 2018 top. In 2022, on the other hand, gas only started leaving the tank at an RSI of 91, which would leave some more room for appreciation.

Conclusion

Pilbara Minerals is not as attractive as before but retains upside potential from long-term lithium price appreciation due to surging demand. Investors should lock in some of the gains and continue riding the trend with the rest. This investment could also serve as a hedge against inflation and a downturn similar to 2022, during which markets bottomed but lithium stocks topped.

Rating: Downgrade to Hold.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: VT: A Core Holding, But Not A Conviction Buy
data.description:
With the US dollar trading down and uncertainty surrounding US equities, 2025 saw an increase in interest for global ETFs, leading to record inflows.

Previously, I compared two global ETFs from State Street: the SPDR® Portfolio MSCI Global Stock Market ETF (SPGM) and State Street® SPDR® Global Dow ETF (DGT). As a continuation of my previous research on global ETFs, I now turn to the Vanguard Total World Stock Index Fund ETF (VT).

With $76.58B in AUM, VT is significantly larger than the comparable ETFs from State Street. That being said, I aim to quantify the fundamental differences and compare valuation and technical metrics to other ETFs in its peer group. In doing so, I will conclude with my Buy/Sell/Hold recommendation.

Fund Overview

VT has an inception dating back to June 2008, providing nearly two decades of performance and fund management to consider. According to the fund's website, the fund's objective is:

"To track the performance of the FTSE Global All Cap Index, which covers well-established and still-developing markets."

In doing so, the fund invests in both foreign and U.S. stocks. Vanguard states that the fund has high-growth potential, as well as high risk, and is best suited for long-term investors.

According to Seeking Alpha, its peers consist of iShares MSCI Kokusai ETF (TOK), SPDR® Portfolio MSCI Global Stock Market ETF (SPGM), SPDR® MSCI World StrategicFactors ETF (QWLD), iShares MSCI Global Min Vol Factor ETF (ACWV), and iShares Global 100 ETF (IOO).

Furthermore, having been around since 1975, Vanguard is one of the top investment management firms in the world.

The Index Screening Process

As mentioned, VT tracks the FTSE Global All Cap Index, a market-cap weighted index including approximately 10,000 large, mid, and small-cap global stocks. Provided by FTSE Russell (part of the London Stock Exchange Group), it is one of the most comprehensive benchmarks for global equity.

The index incorporates a free-float market-cap weighted methodology, meaning companies with larger market capitalizations have greater representation. Additionally, the index includes minimum size thresholds, liquidity screens, and country classification rules to determine developed and emerging market status.

It is similar to the MSCI ACWI IMI and other global equity indexes in that a large number of stocks are included (typically in the thousands).

Holdings & Performance

Top Holdings

VT's top holdings include well-known names such as NVIDIA (NVDA), Apple (AAPL), Microsoft (MSFT), Amazon (AMZN), and Google (GOOGL), among others. With the underlying index being market-cap weighted, it makes sense that the top ten holdings make up 21.75% of total holdings. In this regard, VT is similar to many of its peers. For example, SPGM has a similar allocation structure with many of the same names appearing in the top holdings. This differs from more discretionary allocated screening processes seen in funds like DGT, which have more equal diversification throughout the portfolio.

Sector & Region Allocation

VT is again comparable to peers such as SPGM in terms of its sector and region allocation.

As for sector allocation, holdings are highly concentrated in the technology (26.49%) and financials (17.08%) sectors. Industries like industrials and consumer cyclicals make up 11.13% and 10.46% of holdings, with other sectors each maintaining less than 10%.

We saw a nearly identical holding structure when doing our analysis of SPGM. Investors should consider their own existing exposure to technology and financials before allocating to VT, as the fund offers global diversification while still maintaining many US blue-chip names that may already appear in one's portfolio.

In terms of geographical diversification, 62.50% of holdings are in US equities. While the fund is heavily concentrated in US stocks, this is broadly in line with global equity market weighting as a whole.

For investors looking for lower levels of US exposure, funds like DGT offer slightly less (approximately 55%) while still being globally diversified. Additionally, funds like DGT and IOO offer less concentration in the technology sector for investors already with such exposure.

Recent Performance

The price of VT is up 22.31% on a YoY basis, outperforming the S&P 500 (SPY) by roughly 5 percentage points. With that said, VT has performed broadly in line with its peer group, with IOO and SPGM posting marginally higher 1-year returns (27.53% and 23.39% compared to 22.31%).

This is also true when looking at total returns. VT outperforms ACWV, QWLD, and TOK, while again underperforming IOO and SPGM (Total 1-year returns = 24.72%).

Valuation

On a comparative valuation basis, VT appears to be neither cheap nor overly expensive, falling in the middle of its peer group for most valuation metrics. While VT has the largest AUM [by a considerable margin], price/earnings, price/book, price/sales, and price/cash flow scores slightly higher than those of IOO and TOK. VT's valuation ratios rank closest to that of SPGM.

VT

QWLD

(Source: Fidelity)

Technicals

VT is currently trading at a weekly RSI of 70.40, suggesting that while investors remain bullish, optimism may soon reach a peak. I think, given the high RSI levels, investors should be cautious of a pullback towards the middle-RSI region. Having tested the lower band in April of 2025, I would like to see a pullback before allocating to the fund.

Additionally, the fund is currently trading above its 20-week, 50-week, and 200-week simple moving averages. In the past, VT has routinely tested the 200-week SMA. For example, in June 2022, January, March, and October 2023, and April 2025, the 200-week SMA has served as support.

The price of VT is currently trading at approximately 40% above the 200-week SMA.

With RSI in overbought territory and prices significantly above moving averages, I think investors should be cautious before opening a position. In my opinion, I would like to see VT return nearer to its moving averages (historical region of support) before allocating to a new position.

Additional Factors & Risks

VT receives strong Seeking Alpha Momentum (B+), Expense (A+), and Dividend scores (B+).

With 17 years of dividend payments and 3 consecutive years of dividend growth, the current dividend yield TTM (1.78%) slightly underperforms compared to the median of all other ETFs. In my view, VT does provide an income component for investors specifically seeking global diversification; however, income-specific investors will likely find better returns elsewhere.

Where Vanguard as a whole generally shines is in its low expenses.

VT's expense ratio compares notably better when compared to its peer group. With an expense ratio of 0.06%, this ranks better than SPGM and all other ETFs we have considered in our comparison.

ETF

Expense Ratio

VT

SPGM

ACWV

TOK

0.25%

QWLD

0.30%

IOO

0.40%

DGT

0.50%

(Source: Seeking Alpha)

In terms of potential negative factors, while VT compares similarly to its peers in allocation structure, concentration risk remains prevalent. For investors seeking more international holdings and less focus on US equities, they may consider funds such as DGT or IQLT.

Additionally, as I have outlined in my other articles on global ETFs, it is foreseeable that if the dollar were to recover to previous levels seen in 2024, inflows into both internationally focused and globally diversified ETFs could waver.

With a 62.50/37.50% split between US and foreign equities, a scenario exists in which US markets underperform and/or international markets underperform. In such a scenario, it is foreseeable that VT may underperform the broad market.

It is also worth noting that for investors seeking to capitalize on continued dollar weakness and the potential for foreign market returns exceeding domestic markets, I believe that they would be better off allocating to specifically internationally focused ETFs (such as IQLT).

Conclusion

VT is up 22.31% YoY, outperforming the S&P 500 (SPY), and performing similarly to its peers such as TOK, SPGM, QWLD, ACWV, and IOO.

VT maintains many similar characteristics to SPGM (value, technicals, holdings, and performance) while having a slightly better expense ratio. For long-term investors implementing a DCA strategy, I think VT is a worthy addition to a diversified portfolio. With that said, for investors waiting for an entry point, the 200-week SMA has historically served as an area of support.

All in all, I do not see any significant factors differentiating VT from its peers. While I think it has a valuable place in a portfolio, I also think that short-term price action could be muted compared to what was seen in 2025. This is further supported by the fact that VT has historically underperformed the S&P 500.

Based on my analysis, I consider VT a "Hold". The fund will likely continue to perform well if the broad market generally performs well. Investors should continue to monitor the US Dollar Index (DXY), fund inflows, valuation metrics, and relative performance.

data.title: EUR/USD Hints A Breakout After Latest Trump-Greenland Chaos (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
By Elior Manier

In case you missed the headlines, the attention quickly shifted from a potential intervention in Iran to heightened US threats to acquire Greenland by purchase.

After the threats over the weekend, EU heads of state are planning an emergency meeting, even as the World Economic Forum in Davos begins.

The recent geopolitical intimidation against Denmark and Greenland, combined with additional economic warnings, has prompted the European Union to raise the current 15% tariff rate by 10% if the European Union disagrees, starting February 1.

Despite American markets being closed today for MLK Day, US assets have sold off quite harshly, with the latest tariff and general Trump volatility hurting the US dollar.

On the other hand, the European Central Bank is consolidating its power and stability as Vice President de Guindos officially steps down, with hawk-leaning Croatian Governor Boris Vujcic selected as his replacement.

With the latest events and flows, EUR:USD is breaking to the upside and could attract quite a bit of attention (and volatility) for the times to come - keep a close eye on these developments.

In the meantime, let's dive into a multi-time frame EUR/USD technical analysis.

EUR/USD Multi-Time Frame Technical Analysis

Daily Chart

EUR/USD is attempting an upside breakout from its end-2025 descending channel.

Bouncing off of its 200-Day Moving Average (which just caught up from the 10% 2025 rally in the pair), the immediate flows and events could cause a larger breakout from its 6-month long 4,000 pip consolidation.

Several hurdles will need to be breached before that.

  • The immediate test comes around 1.1630, which acts as key momentum pivot and coincides with the actual breakout from the channel.
  • Breaking and closing 1.18 on the weekly could test the 1.20 levels. Such developments would take more time
  • Such scenarios exclude a potential of Trump backing off of his recent words - the best scenario for the USD.

4H Chart and Technical Levels

Watch if tomorrow closes above or below the 4H-50 MA to confirm a breakout or rejection of the channel higher bound.

Resistance levels

  • 1.1640-1.1660 intermediate pivot and 4H 50-MA (1.16490)
  • 1.17 psychological level
  • 1.1750 minor resistance
  • Main resistance 1.18 (range highs)

Support levels

  • 1.1580-1.16 key support
  • 1.1550 channel lows
  • 1.1470-1.15 pivotal support (range lows)

1H Chart

EUR/USD shows a more balanced price action as volumes largely fall off (US traders are off).

It will be very interesting to see whether bulls push for a (descending) channel breakout or the 1H 200 MA/4H 50 MA stalls the price action.

Rejecting the channel highs would point either to a retest of Sunday lows (1.15780) or continued downside (lower odds looking at the current situation).

Note: The euro could still be affected negatively from the current development, reason why the Swiss franc is leading the daily FX flows.

Safe trades!

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Longleaf Partners Small-Cap Fund Q4 2025 Portfolio Activity, Contributors & Detractors
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the Longleaf Partners Small-Cap Fund 2025 Annual Commentary.

Notable Contributors & Detractors

Beclé – Leading manufacturer of tequila and whiskeys Beclé contributed this year. The company was severely undervalued in the first quarter when we took advantage of a share price dislocation to purchase during peak US/Mexico tariff uncertainty. The company has strong brands that we know well having previously owned the company in our non-US strategy, even though most of the value now comes from the US despite the company's Mexican listing. Beclé's current undervaluation is further complicated by the market's cautious outlook on the alcohol industry, which has continued throughout the year (and led to us adding to the position in the second half of the year). The company has aligned partners in the Beckmann family, and owning roughly 30% of the growing, global tequila market with 200+ year old brands at 10x or less FCF power positions us well for the long-term.

GCI Liberty (GLIBA) – Alaskan communications company GCI Liberty was a positive contributor for the year. We purchased GCI after it spun out of Liberty Broadband (LBRDA) in July. GCI holds the number one position in both consumer and enterprise broadband in Alaska, while also owning the second largest wireless network. Their assets are essentially irreplicable due to the location, climate, and terrain of Alaska. A combination of attractive tax attributes from the spin and a business that will be producing significant FCF in the near term which could allow GCI to be the next iteration of Liberty Media (FWONA). All-time great Southeastern partner John Malone will remain Chairman at GCI despite stepping back from most other boards, and he has shown his belief in the company through both insider buys and fully backstopping a recently completed $300 million rights offering. The stock appreciated as the market better understood it post-spin, but the company still trades at a sizable discount to Lower 48 telecom assets despite having a much better competitive position.

Graham Holdings (GHC) – Diversified conglomerate GHC performed strongly throughout the year. The company's Kaplan education segment has multiple subparts that are finally back to growth in aggregate after a multi-year turnaround has borne fruit. It is encouraging that broadcast television M&A has increased this year, which could give GHC a unique opportunity to grow and realize its value in this segment. The healthcare segment continued its strong growth and looks to be navigating a leadership change well. Overall, GHC remains on offense with its strong net cash balance sheet and overfunded pension. Our P/V Podcast with CEO Tim O'Shaughnessy from earlier this year remains a good listen if you would like to learn more on the company.

Mattel (MAT) – Children's toy, media, and consumer products creator Mattel was a contributor for the quarter and the year. The company is in its strongest position in over 10 years, and there are multiple ways to win. Over 80% of Mattel's value comes from growing power brands like Hot Wheels, Barbie, and UNO. Mattel has a strong balance sheet which allowed material stock repurchases of $600 million in 2025, and we believe additional share repurchase will come at these discounted prices in 2026. Fundamentally, the toy business continues to grow and gross margins remain strong at 50%. Mattel has a promising owned IP (intellectual property) outlook for 2026 with the Masters of the Universe and Matchbox movies, along with two video games, being released.

Oscar Health (OSCR) – Healthcare insurance company Oscar was a contributor for the year as above-market membership growth and sustained expense discipline reinforced confidence in the company's positioning within the individual health insurance market. We exited our position in the third quarter as the P/V gap closed and industry risks became more apparent. The subsequent drama around the expiration of enhanced Affordable Care Act (ACA) subsidies underscores the ongoing policy uncertainty and challenges facing ACA-centric insurers like Oscar. Overall, the investment was successful for us, aided by management's execution through a volatile industry backdrop.

Boston Beer (SAM) – Alcoholic beverage company Boston Beer was a detractor for the year as alcohol consumption faced headwinds. Volume declines at Twisted Tea, the company's largest brand, weighed on the stock. Twisted's pricing had gotten a little overextended, but the company is actively working to fix this problem and has also successfully fended off numerous competitors that entered the hard tea space over the last few years. Additionally, Truly is still having difficulty returning to growth after multiple difficult years but is now a much smaller part of the company. Boston Beer has continually proven itself as one of the best innovators in the industry, as shown again by its new and successful vodka-based product Sun Cruiser, which is in the process of a national rollout and will be key to getting the company back to growth. We have been pleased by the speed at which management has corrected margin problems that arose in the aftermath of the hard seltzer boom, and we still believe there is further room for improvement. Founder Jim Koch stepped back into the CEO seat in August for the first time in almost 25 years after former CEO Michael Spillane stepped down. The company still has a net cash position and is one of our largest share repurchasers. We think repurchase is a great use of capital for a consumer packaged goods company with quality brands that trades at 1x revenue, all at a time that it could be an acquisition target amidst industry consolidation.

Clearwater Paper (CLW) – Packaging company Clearwater Paper was a detractor for the year. We bought shares of Clearwater in April. The stock has since sold off as the already weak solid bleached sulfate (SBS) paperboard market showed few signs of improving. The market has been depressed and below mid-cycle due to oversupply problems that were exacerbated by a competitor conversion earlier this year. Clearwater continues to operate well what it can control, as evidenced by meeting synergy targets from a large and opportunistic acquisition completed last year. The clearest way the oversupply problem will be fixed is through capacity closures, but help is also coming in the form of tariffs on competing European capacity, as well as a weaker dollar that makes these imports less appealing. Management has a strong capital allocation track record and recently decided to focus on share repurchase after running an analysis on a high return but time-consuming project. We agree with this decision as the business trades at a low single-digit multiple of growing mid-cycle FCF.

Empire State Realty (ESRT) and Alexander's (ALX) – In the fourth quarter of 2024, we were trimming our position in New York real estate company ESRT, as it had been a strong performer and the P/V gap had closed. This year, both ESRT and its peer Alexander's (which was a new position in 2025) faced stock market headwinds as New York real estate sentiment shifted. We have added to both at great prices. It is an interesting dynamic where the on-the-ground results are stronger than the headlines, with region-wide leasing trends far outpacing weak stock price performance. The stock market worries about Mayor Mamdani, but that fear is now in the stock prices, plus it remains to be seen how truly business-unfriendly his administration will be. New York real estate has made it through a lot (the city going broke in the 1970s, 9/11, COVID, etc.) historically. These two stocks traded at or above our appraisals during the not-great de Blasio administration and are uniquely undervalued vs. peers today. Both of our holdings are on offense with multiple ways to grow value per share and a willingness to sell assets into a more attractively priced private market.

Park Hotels & Resorts (PK) – Hotel REIT Park Hotels & Resorts was a detractor for the year. The company sold off due to worries about how an uneasy macro environment will impact a leisure-focused hotel company. The iconic Hilton Hawaiian Village, Park's most valuable property, is still recovering from both a labor strike in 2024 and reduced Japanese inbound travel as a result of the weaker yen. The company has continued down the path of selling non-core hotels and has thus far received attractive prices, while share repurchases have also occurred. We sold our position earlier in the year due to a combination of recycling capital into better ideas and never getting our position to a normal weight.

Portfolio Activity

After a more active first nine months of the year, we had no new purchases during the quarter while we exited our Kodak position as noted above.

During the year we purchased seven new holdings: Shenandoah Telecommunications (SHEN), TripAdvisor (TRIP), Becl, Alexander's, Clearwater Paper, GCI Liberty and Rayonier. In addition to Kodak, we exited seven other holdings: Anywhere Real Estate, Liberty Live (LLYVA), Howard Hughes (HHH), Hyatt (H), Oscar Health, Park Hotels and Westrock Coffee (WEST).

RISKS

The Longleaf Small-Cap Fund is subject to stock market risk, meaning stocks in the Fund may fluctuate in response to developments at individual companies or due to general market and economic conditions. Also, because the Fund generally invests in 15 to 25 companies, share value could fluctuate more than if a greater number of securities were held. Smaller company stocks may be more volatile with less financial resources than those of larger companies.

The Russell 2000 Index measures the performance of the 2000 smallest companies in the Russell 3000 Index. The Russell 3000 represents approximately 97% of the American public equity market and the Russell 2000 represents 10% of the Russell 3000. An index is unmanaged, does not reflect the deduction of fees or expenses, and cannot be invested in directly.

P/V ("price to value") is a calculation that compares the prices of the stocks in a portfolio to Southeastern's appraisal of their intrinsic values. The ratio represents a single data point about a Fund and should not be construed as something more. P/V does not guarantee future results, and we caution investors not to give this calculation undue weight.

"Margin of Safety" is a reference to the difference between a stock's market price and Southeastern's calculated appraisal value. It is not a guarantee of investment performance or returns.

Free Cash Flow (FCF) is a measure of a company's ability to generate the cash flow necessary to maintain operations. Generally, it is calculated as operating cash flow minus capital expenditures.

Price / Earnings (P/E) is the ratio of a company's share price compared to its earnings per share.

Enterprise value (EV) is a company's market capitalization plus debt, minority interest and preferred shares, and less total cash and cash equivalents.

PE multiple is a financial metric that frames a company's current stock price in terms of the company's earnings per share.

REIT is a Real Estate Investment Trust which is a company that owns, operates, or finances income-producing real estate.

A Basis Point is one hundredth of one percent.

Mergers and Acquisitions (M&A) are business transactions in which the ownership of a company, business organization, or one of their operating units is transferred to or consolidated with another entity.

As of December 31, 2025, the top ten holdings for the Longleaf Partners Small-Cap Fund: White Mountains, 7.5%; GCI Liberty, 7.2%; Becl, 6.8%; Mattel, 6.0%; CNX Resources, 5.8%; TripAdvisor, 5.7%; Gruma 5.3%; Shenandoah Telecommunications 4.7%, PotlatchDeltic, 4.7% and Graham Holdings, 4.5%. Fund holdings are subject to change and holdings discussions are not recommendations to buy or sell any security. Current and future holdings are subject to risk.
